description: Enriched metadata output of the MMS Parser (Enricher). Designed to train AI agents for airline MRO operations.

enricher.process: 6-stage enrichment pipeline: name_enrichment → domain_mapping → relationship_discovery → glossary_compliance → usecase_process_gen → decision_alert_gen

entity_types.description: One per database table. Adds business context: what feature it supports, why it exists, which endpoints hit it, what processes use it.

fields.name: entity_id
fields.description: Unique identifier

fields.name: source_table
fields.description: Physical table name in source database

fields.name: source_schema
fields.description: Database schema

fields.name: source_database
fields.description: Source database type (postgresql, cosmos, oracle, etc.)

fields.name: business_name
fields.description: Human-readable table name

fields.name: business_description
fields.description: Plain-English explanation of what this table holds

fields.name: feature
fields.description: Which MMS business feature this table supports (from mro_features.json)

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Why this table exists — the business justification / why the airline needs this data

fields.name: domain
fields.description: TOGAF business domain (e.g. Fleet Management, Maintenance Execution, Inventory, Compliance, Workforce, Analytics)

fields.name: regulatory_significance
fields.description: EASA/FAA/ICAO regulations that mandate or govern this table

fields.name: stakeholders
fields.description: Roles that use this data

fields.name: key_processes
fields.description: Business processes this table enables (e.g. Aircraft Induction, Check Scheduling, CRS Issuance, Lease Return)

fields.name: endpoints
fields.description: API endpoints that read/write this table

fields.name: columns
fields.description: Array of enriched_column objects

fields.name: foreign_key_relationships
fields.description: FK links to other tables with business meaning

fields.name: sample_agent_queries
fields.description: Questions an agent might ask + the SQL to answer them

entity_types.description: One per column. Adds business meaning, enum expansions, sensitivity, and agent behavior notes.

fields.name: description
fields.description: What this column means in business terms

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Why this column is captured — what operational or regulatory need it serves

fields.name: is_safety_critical
fields.description: If true, this field carries safety implications for airworthiness

fields.name: enum_values
fields.description: If enum, list all values with their business meaning

fields.name: agent_notes
fields.description: Guidance to AI agent on how to interpret or use this column

entity_types.description: Foreign key and implicit business relationships between tables.

fields.name: business_meaning
fields.description: What this relationship means in the real world (e.g. 'An aircraft has multiple maintenance visits over its lifetime')

entity_types.description: One per API endpoint. Maps to features, tables, business rules, and authorization requirements.

fields.name: use_case
fields.description: When and why someone calls this endpoint

fields.name: feature
fields.description: Which MMS feature this endpoint implements

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Why this endpoint exists — the business or regulatory need it fulfills

fields.name: related_tables
fields.description: Which database tables this endpoint reads or writes

fields.name: business_rules
fields.description: Validation rules and business logic enforced at this endpoint

fields.name: side_effects
fields.description: What else changes when this endpoint is called (e.g. status updates)

fields.name: agent_notes
fields.description: Guidance to AI agent on how to handle this endpoint

entity_types.description: End-to-end business process that spans tables and endpoints.

fields.name: feature
fields.description: Which MMS feature this process belongs to

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Why this process exists — the operational or regulatory driver

fields.name: steps
fields.description: Ordered steps, each with system_actions (endpoint calls), performed_by (roles), and business_rules

entity_types.description: Domain-specific terms, acronyms, regulatory references. The agent must know these to speak the airline's language.

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Why this concept matters in airline MRO

fields.name: agent_notes
fields.description: Behavioral guidance for AI agent when this term is encountered

entity_types.description: Concrete business scenario the agent will be asked to handle.

fields.name: feature
fields.description: Which MMS feature this use case supports

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Why this use case is important to the airline

tables.business_description: Master record of every aircraft in the airline's fleet. One row per tail number. Contains identity, model assignment, current operational status, ownership type (owned/leased), and current base station.

feature.description: Project next due dates for scheduled checks based on projected fleet utilization, seasonality, and historical turnaround patterns
feature.reason: Enables long-term hangar slot booking, labor resource allocation, and lease return condition planning months in advance

tables.reason: The aircraft table is the root entity of the entire MMS. Every maintenance visit, task card, defect record, compliance entry, and inventory movement traces back to a specific aircraft. Without this table, no maintenance work can be planned, executed, or recorded.
regulatory_significance: FAA Part 121.153 - Aircraft registration and eligibility
ICAO Annex 6 Part I - Operation of aircraft
key_processes: Aircraft Induction - Adding a new aircraft to the fleet register
Maintenance Visit Planning - Scheduling checks against an aircraft
Fleet Status Monitoring - Real-time view of airworthiness across the fleet
Lease Return - Managing end-of-lease maintenance obligations
AOG Response - Emergency escalation when aircraft goes AOG

endpoints.purpose: List all aircraft with filters (status, model, station)
endpoints.use_case: Operations center views fleet status at a glance

endpoints.purpose: Get full aircraft details including engines and active defects
endpoints.use_case: Dispatcher checks airworthiness before scheduling flight
Flight ops system polls whether aircraft can dispatch

columns.description: System-generated UUID. Internal key used for all foreign key references across the database. Not displayed to users.
columns.reason: Provides a stable, immutable identifier for the aircraft regardless of tail number changes (re-registrations happen during lease transfers). All 37 other tables reference this ID for relational integrity.
columns.agent_notes: Use for JOINs only. Never display to users. Use tail_number for human-facing responses.

columns.description: Aircraft registration mark displayed on the fuselage. Format: 2-letter country prefix + 3-4 alphanumeric characters. Example: G-EZAT (UK), N123AB (USA), D-AIBC (Germany), VT-XXX (India).
columns.reason: The tail number is the universal identifier used by pilots, ATC, ground handlers, and regulators. It links the physical aircraft to its digital record. Required on all regulatory documentation.
columns.agent_notes: Primary human-readable identifier. Use in all user-facing responses. Example: 'G-EZAT is an A320-251N based at LGW.'

columns.description: Current airworthiness and availability state of the entire aircraft. Drives whether the aircraft can be dispatched for commercial service.
columns.reason: This is the single most important operational field. It determines flight dispatch legality, insurance coverage, and hangar slot allocation. AOG status triggers airline-wide emergency protocols.

enum_values.meaning: Flying or available for flight. All maintenance current, CRS valid. Dispatch OK.
In hangar for scheduled/unscheduled work. Not available. Dispatch NOT OK.
Aircraft on Ground — technical issue prevents flight. Emergency status. Highest priority for all departments. Dispatch NOT OK.
Long-term storage/preservation. Requires reactivation checks. Dispatch NOT OK.
Permanently removed from service. Dispatch NOT OK.

columns.agent_notes: CRITICAL: Before answering any 'can this aircraft fly' question, check this field. If status != ACTIVE, the answer is NO. AOG is a declared emergency — escalate immediately.

columns.description: OEM-assigned serial number unique to the airframe. Set at final assembly and never changes for the life of the aircraft.
columns.reason: Required for regulatory tracking, AD applicability checking (by MSN range), lease return condition verification, and accident investigation. Unlike tail_number, MSN never changes.

foreign_key_relationships.business_meaning: An aircraft has exactly one model definition (A320-251N, B737-8, etc). The model determines which maintenance program applies, which parts are compatible, and which mechanic type ratings are required.
The airport/base where the aircraft is currently positioned. Changes daily based on flight schedule.
An aircraft has many maintenance visits over its lifetime. The active visit (status IN_PROGRESS) tells you where the aircraft is right now.
An aircraft has many life-limited parts installed. Each LLP tracks its own remaining life.
An aircraft can have multiple deferred (MEL) defects active simultaneously.
If leased, links to the lease agreement which governs return conditions, maintenance obligations, and redelivery milestones.

sample_agent_queries.question: How many aircraft are currently AOG?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM aircraft WHERE status = 'AOG'

sample_agent_queries.question: Show me all A320-family aircraft and their current status
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT a.tail_number, a.status, m.model_name FROM aircraft a JOIN aircraft_model m ON a.model_id = m.model_id WHERE m.model_name LIKE 'A320%'

sample_agent_queries.question: Which aircraft are due for C-check in the next 30 days?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT a.tail_number, mv.planned_start_date, mv.visit_reference FROM aircraft a JOIN maintenance_visit mv ON a.aircraft_id = mv.aircraft_id WHERE mv.check_type = 'C_CHECK' AND mv.status IN ('PLANNED','CONFIRMED') AND mv.planned_start_date BETWEEN CURRENT_DATE AND CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '30 days' ORDER BY mv.planned_start_date

sample_agent_queries.question: What is the current lease status of G-EZAT?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT a.tail_number, l.lessor_name, l.start_date, l.end_date, l.return_conditions FROM aircraft a JOIN lease_agreement l ON a.aircraft_id = l.aircraft_id WHERE a.tail_number = 'G-EZAT'

tables.business_description: Each row represents one scheduled or unscheduled maintenance visit for an aircraft. Captures the full lifecycle: planned → confirmed → in_progress → completed. Links the aircraft, check package, station, all task cards, and the resulting Certificate of Release to Service.

feature.description: Define and manage A/B/C/D check packages, interval triggers (flight hours, cycles, calendar), and task scope per aircraft model and variant
feature.reason: Ensures each aircraft adheres to manufacturer-approved maintenance programs and prevents unscheduled groundings

tables.reason: The maintenance visit is the central organizing entity for all maintenance execution. It packages hundreds of task cards into a single operation, tracks cost and labor at the visit level, and produces the CRS that proves airworthiness. Without visits, maintenance would be unmanaged and untraceable.
regulatory_significance: EASA Part 145.A.50 - Maintenance records and release
FAA Part 43.9 - Content of maintenance records
key_processes: Check Scheduling - Creating planned visits based on interval forecasts
Visit Execution - Managing the hangar operation from induction to sign-off
CRS Generation - Issuing the legal certificate that returns the aircraft to service
Maintenance Cost Accrual - Tracking labor hours, parts consumed, and vendor costs per visit
Lease Return Preparation - Ensuring all visit records are complete for redelivery

endpoints.purpose: List all visits with filters (aircraft, status, check type, date range)
Create a new visit. Auto-generates task cards from the check package.
Get visit details including task card status, findings, and CRS reference
Start the visit. Aircraft enters hangar. Status changes to IN_PROGRESS.
Complete the visit. All tasks done, CRS issued. Status changes to COMPLETED.
Get all task cards for this visit with status per card

columns.description: Human-readable reference code. Format: MV-2026-0001 (MV = Maintenance Visit, year, sequential number). Used on all physical documentation, hangar whiteboards, and communication.
columns.reason: Provides a human-friendly identifier for the visit that mechanics, planners, and auditors can reference without UUIDs. Essential for cross-department communication and hangar floor operations.
columns.agent_notes: Use this reference when communicating with users about a visit. Example: 'MV-2026-0142 is the C-check on G-EZAT currently in progress at LGW hangar 3.'

columns.description: Classification of the maintenance check being performed. Determines the scope, duration, cost, and resource requirements for the visit.
columns.reason: Check type is the primary driver of resource planning. C-checks need hangar space for 2 weeks and 3000+ man-hours. A-checks can be done overnight. Misclassifying a check causes cascading scheduling failures.

enum_values.meaning: Light check every 500-800 flight hours. Overnight or 1-2 days. ~50 man-hours.
Intermediate check. Usually absorbed into A-check scope. ~150 man-hours.
Heavy check every 18-24 months. ~3000 man-hours. Requires dedicated hangar bay.
Heaviest check every 6-10 years. Full strip and structural inspection. ~50000 man-hours.
C-check tasks split across multiple A-check slots to minimize downtime. Common in low-cost carriers.
Emergency or defect-driven. No standard duration or scope.

columns.agent_notes: Check type drives everything: cost estimate, hangar booking duration, parts kitting, and mechanic scheduling. A D_CHECK cannot be done in an overnight slot. C_CHECK in 3 days means a phased approach is needed.

columns.description: Current stage in the visit lifecycle. Controls what actions are allowed and what data is visible.
columns.reason: Status enforces the maintenance workflow. You cannot generate a CRS until status is COMPLETED. You cannot start a visit that is still PLANNED. Status prevents out-of-sequence operations that could compromise airworthiness records.

enum_values.meaning: Visit created in system with planned dates. No physical work. Can be rescheduled.
Hangar slot locked, resources committed, aircraft confirmed for input date.
Aircraft in hangar. Task cards being executed. Status changes cost tracking.
All task cards done/def erred. CRS issued. Aircraft returned to service.
Visit cancelled before work started. Slots freed for other use.

columns.agent_notes: Only COMPLETED visits have a valid CRS. An IN_PROGRESS visit means the aircraft is unavailable. If someone asks 'when will the aircraft be ready?', check the visit status and actual progress.

foreign_key_relationships.business_meaning: Each visit is for exactly one aircraft. An aircraft has many visits over its lifetime.
Each visit follows a specific check package that defines the task scope.
A visit has many task cards (one per maintenance task in the package). Each task card tracks a single mechanic's work.
A completed visit generates exactly one CRS. The CRS is the legal output of the visit.

sample_agent_queries.question: Which aircraft are currently in the hangar for maintenance?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT a.tail_number, mv.visit_reference, mv.check_type, mv.planned_end_date FROM maintenance_visit mv JOIN aircraft a ON mv.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id WHERE mv.status = 'IN_PROGRESS' ORDER BY mv.planned_end_date

sample_agent_queries.question: How many C-checks are scheduled for next quarter?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM maintenance_visit WHERE check_type = 'C_CHECK' AND status IN ('PLANNED','CONFIRMED') AND planned_start_date BETWEEN date_trunc('quarter', CURRENT_DATE) AND date_trunc('quarter', CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '3 months')

sample_agent_queries.question: What is the average turnaround time for C-checks this year?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT AVG(actual_end_date - actual_start_date) AS avg_days FROM maintenance_visit WHERE check_type = 'C_CHECK' AND status = 'COMPLETED' AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM actual_start_date) = EXTRACT(YEAR FROM CURRENT_DATE)

sample_agent_queries.question: Show me overdue maintenance visits
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT mv.visit_reference, a.tail_number, mv.check_type, mv.planned_end_date, mv.actual_end_date FROM maintenance_visit mv JOIN aircraft a ON mv.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id WHERE mv.status = 'IN_PROGRESS' AND mv.planned_end_date < CURRENT_DATE ORDER BY mv.planned_end_date

tables.business_description: Tracks every life-limited component (LLP) by serial number. Each row represents one physical part instance installed on an aircraft or in storage. Records total life limit (in cycles, hours, or calendar), remaining life, and installation/removal history.

feature.description: Track parts with fixed life limits by serial number, monitor remaining hours/cycles/calendar time, and enforce removal before expiry
feature.reason: Prevents catastrophic failures and ensures full traceability for regulatory audits and asset resale value

tables.reason: LLPs have hard mandatory life limits imposed by the OEM and regulator. Exceeding an LLP limit can cause catastrophic in-flight failure (e.g. uncontained engine failure, landing gear collapse). This table ensures parts are removed before limits expire, and provides the audit trail required by EASA Part 21 and FAA Part 39 for every LLP on every aircraft.
key_processes: LLP Installation - Recording a new or overhauled LLP installed on an aircraft
Life Consumption Tracking - Automatically decrementing remaining life based on flight utilization
LLP Removal Alerting - Warning at 50%, 90%, and 100% of life consumed
Shop Visit Coordination - Sending LLP for overhaul before limit expires
Component Retirement - Removing LLP from service permanently at end of life

endpoints.purpose: List all LLPs with filters (aircraft, status, threshold alert)
Get replacement forecast showing when this LLP will hit its limit

columns.description: OEM-stamped serial number unique to this physical part instance. Used for traceability from manufacture through every installation and removal.
columns.reason: Serial number is the key to back-to-birth traceability. Every LLP has a unique identity that must be tracked from the OEM production line to final retirement. Regulators require full traceability for accident investigation.

columns.description: Computed value showing remaining flight cycles, flight hours, or calendar days before the part must be removed. Unit varies by part type.
columns.reason: This is the critical decision field. When remaining_life reaches zero, the part is expired and the aircraft is grounded. Proactive planning requires knowing remaining life at all times to schedule removals before expiry.
columns.agent_notes: CRITICAL: If remaining_life <= 0, the part is EXPIRED. The aircraft cannot fly until replaced. Check this field before answering any question about aircraft availability.

columns.description: Maximum permitted life before mandatory removal. Set by the OEM and approved by the regulator. Never changes for a given part type.
columns.reason: The total life limit is the regulatory maximum. Part type certification specifies this value. It cannot be extended without re-certification.

columns.description: Generated column: ((total_life_limit - remaining_life) / total_life_limit) * 100. Used for threshold alerting.
columns.reason: Percentage provides a normalized view across different LLP types with different life limits. Enables consistent alerting at 50%, 90%, and 100% thresholds regardless of whether the unit is cycles, hours, or days.

foreign_key_relationships.business_meaning: Each LLP is installed on one aircraft at a time. Over its lifetime, an LLP may move between aircraft (but this is rare).
Each LLP is an instance of a part type defined in the part master catalog.

sample_agent_queries.question: Which LLPs are approaching their life limit (over 90% consumed)?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT llp.part_serial_number, p.part_number, p.part_name, llp.remaining_life, llp.total_life_limit, ((llp.total_life_limit - llp.remaining_life)::decimal / llp.total_life_limit) * 100 AS pct_consumed, a.tail_number FROM life_limited_part llp JOIN part_master p ON llp.part_master_id = p.part_id JOIN aircraft a ON llp.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id WHERE ((llp.total_life_limit - llp.remaining_life)::decimal / llp.total_life_limit) * 100 >= 90 ORDER BY pct_consumed DESC

sample_agent_queries.question: Are there any expired LLPs on active aircraft?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT a.tail_number, llp.part_serial_number, p.part_number FROM life_limited_part llp JOIN aircraft a ON llp.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id JOIN part_master p ON llp.part_master_id = p.part_id WHERE llp.remaining_life <= 0 AND a.status = 'ACTIVE'

sample_agent_queries.question: When does the HPT blade on engine 1 of G-EZAT need replacement?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT llp.part_serial_number, llp.remaining_life, llp.total_life_limit FROM life_limited_part llp JOIN aircraft a ON llp.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id JOIN part_master p ON llp.part_master_id = p.part_id WHERE a.tail_number = 'G-EZAT' AND p.part_number LIKE '%HPT-BLADE%' AND llp.installation_position = 'ENG1'

tables.business_description: Tracks every serialized high-value rotable component (e.g. landing gear, actuators, pumps, avionics boxes) through its lifecycle. Each row represents one physical component with its current status, location, installation history, repair history, and pool allocation.

feature.description: Track serialized high-value components through their lifecycle with pool allocation, repair history, and current location across the network
feature.reason: Rotables represent the majority of MRO inventory capital; optimized pool management reduces spare asset investment and prevents stockouts

tables.reason: Rotable components are high-value ($50K-$2M each) items that are repaired rather than discarded. The airline must maintain a pool of serviceable spares to swap when a component fails. Without this table, the airline cannot locate spares, track repair turnaround times, or optimize pool size — leading to either AOG delays or excessive capital tied up in spare inventory.
key_processes: Component Removal - Recording a component removed from an aircraft
Component Installation - Recording a component installed on an aircraft (with traceability)
Repair Order Management - Sending unserviceable components to repair shops
Pool Rebalancing - Moving spares between stations to match demand
Cannibalization - Removing a serviceable component from one aircraft to fix another (requires approval)

endpoints.purpose: List rotable components with filters (part, status, station, serial)
Get component details with full removal/installation/repair history
Move component between stations (for pool rebalancing)
Get pool allocation across all stations
Trigger optimization to redistribute spares based on demand forecast

columns.description: OEM serial number. Unique identifier for this physical component across its entire service life.
columns.reason: Essential for back-to-birth traceability. Each installation and removal must reference the serial number to maintain the component history. Regulatory requirement for accident investigation and warranty claims.

columns.description: Current condition and availability of the component.
columns.reason: Status drives all inventory decisions: serviceable items can be installed, unserviceable items need repair orders, and quarantined items need inspection.

enum_values.meaning: Ready for installation. Has valid release certificate. Available in pool.
Failed or removed due to defect. Needs repair before return to pool.
At external repair shop. Tracked via repair_order.
Suspected defect. Awaiting inspection. Not available for use.
Currently installed on an aircraft.
Beyond economical repair. Permanently removed from pool.

columns.agent_notes: When checking parts availability, only SERVICEABLE status items are usable. INSTALLED_ON_AC items are in use. IN_REPAIR items have uncertain return dates.

foreign_key_relationships.business_meaning: If installed, the component is on this aircraft. Null if in pool storage.
The physical location of the component (hangar, warehouse, or station store).
The part type definition including interchangeability and substitutability rules.

sample_agent_queries.question: Do we have a serviceable IDG for the A320 at LGW?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT rc.serial_number, rc.current_station_id FROM rotable_component rc JOIN part_master p ON rc.part_master_id = p.part_id JOIN station s ON rc.current_station_id = s.station_id WHERE p.part_name LIKE '%IDG%' AND p.aircraft_model LIKE '%A320%' AND rc.status = 'SERVICEABLE' AND s.station_code = 'LGW'

sample_agent_queries.question: How many serviceable spares do we have for part number 1234567?
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM rotable_component WHERE part_master_id = (SELECT part_id FROM part_master WHERE part_number = '1234567') AND status = 'SERVICEABLE'

sample_agent_queries.question: Show me the full installation history of component serial ABC123
sample_agent_queries.sql: SELECT ch.event_date, ch.event_type, ch.aircraft_id, a.tail_number, ch.remarks FROM component_history ch JOIN aircraft a ON ch.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id WHERE ch.component_serial = 'ABC123' ORDER BY ch.event_date DESC

endpoints.description: Creates a new scheduled or unscheduled maintenance visit for an aircraft. Validates aircraft is in ACTIVE status, the check package applies to the aircraft model, and no overlapping visits exist. On success, auto-generates task cards from the check package and sets aircraft status to MAINTENANCE.
endpoints.use_case: Planner schedules a C-check for an aircraft approaching its interval limit, or MCC creates an unscheduled visit when an AOG is declared.

feature.description: Define and manage A/B/C/D check packages, interval triggers, and task scope per aircraft model
feature.reason: Ensures each aircraft adheres to manufacturer-approved maintenance programs and prevents unscheduled groundings

endpoints.reason: This endpoint is the gateway to all maintenance execution. Every hour of hangar work, every mechanic assignment, every part consumed, and every CRS issued starts with a visit created here. It enforces business rules that prevent conflicting schedules and incompatible maintenance scopes.
business_rules: aircraft.status must be ACTIVE to schedule a visit
check_package.model_id must match aircraft.model_id (package must be designed for this model)
No overlapping visits for the same aircraft (dates must not conflict)
planned_end_date must be after planned_start_date
SCHEDULED visits require a check_package_id; UNSCHEDULED visits are created without one
side_effects: Aircraft status changes from ACTIVE to MAINTENANCE
N task cards created (one per task in the selected check package)
Hangar slot consumed for the planned duration
Event logged to Cosmos DB audit trail
endpoints.agent_notes: This is usually the first step in any maintenance story. When creating a visit, the agent must ensure: (1) the aircraft is actually ACTIVE, (2) the package is correct for the model, (3) there is no scheduling conflict. A 409 CONFLICT response means one of these rules failed.

endpoints.business_name: Generate Certificate of Release to Service
endpoints.description: Creates the legal document certifying that all maintenance work has been completed to approved standards and the aircraft is fit for flight. Includes certifying staff identity, scope statement, and digital signature. The CRS is registered in the aircraft's digital logbook.
endpoints.use_case: After a C-check is completed and all task cards are signed off, the certifying engineer generates the CRS to return the aircraft to revenue service.

feature.description: Generate certificates of release to service with authorized signatory validation, digital stamping, and automatic linkage to completed task records
feature.reason: The CRS is the legal document permitting flight after maintenance; invalid or missing certificates ground the aircraft

endpoints.reason: The CRS is the single most important legal document in aircraft maintenance. Without it, the aircraft cannot fly. It proves to the regulator, the airline, and the public that maintenance was performed competently and completely. Every CRS must be signed by properly authorized certifying staff.
business_rules: All task cards for the visit must be COMPLETED or DEFERRED
No open defects classified as GROUNDING on this aircraft
Certifying staff must hold valid EASA Part 66 or FAA A&P license with type rating for this model
Certifying staff cannot sign for work they personally performed (independence rule)
CRS must be generated before the visit can be marked COMPLETED
side_effects: Certificate stored in Cosmos DB maintenance-documents container
Visit status remains COMPLETED after CRS issuance
Logbook entry created recording the CRS reference
Event logged to Cosmos DB audit trail for regulatory review
endpoints.agent_notes: CRITICAL: The CRS is legal permission to fly. Before generating, the agent MUST verify: (1) all task cards are closed, (2) the certifier holds valid type rating, (3) the certifier didn't do the work themselves. A 422 UNPROCESSABLE ENTITY response means the visit has open items.

endpoints.description: Records a deferred defect against an aircraft under MEL (Minimum Equipment List) provisions. Specifies the MEL reference, category (A/B/C/D), operational restrictions, and expiry date. The defect remains active until rectified or expired.
endpoints.use_case: A night-stop mechanic finds an INOP seat actuator. The MEL allows dispatch for 10 days (Category C). The mechanic creates a deferred defect, and the MCC schedules rectification within the limit.

feature.description: Manage minimum equipment list items with categorization, expiry tracking, operational restrictions, and rectification workflow
feature.reason: Allows continued safe operation with inoperative non-critical equipment while ensuring defects are fixed within permitted timeframes

endpoints.reason: Aircraft have thousands of systems. Not every defect grounds the aircraft — the MEL provides a regulated framework to defer non-critical items. This endpoint ensures every deferral is documented, categorized by urgency, and tracked to resolution. Without it, defects would be managed informally and exceed regulatory time limits.
business_rules: Defect must reference a valid MEL item number from the MMEL
MEL category (A/B/C/D) determines maximum deferral duration
Category A defects cannot be extended beyond initial limit
Operational restrictions must be documented (e.g. 'Seat 14F must remain empty')
Cannot defer a defect that affects a safety-critical system (flight controls, engines, landing gear)
Aircraft can dispatch with active defects ONLY if status remains ACTIVE
endpoints.agent_notes: Deferred defects are normal in airline operations, but each has a ticking clock. The agent MUST track expiry dates and alert before they lapse. MEL Category A = urgent (fix in days). Category D = can wait until next check.

processes.process_description: End-to-end process for performing a C-check on an aircraft, from hangar induction through task execution and final CRS issuance. The airline's most resource-intensive maintenance event.

feature.description: Define and manage A/B/C/D check packages, interval triggers, and task scope per aircraft model and variant
feature.reason: Ensures each aircraft adheres to manufacturer-approved maintenance programs and prevents unscheduled groundings

processes.reason: The C-check is the airline's most expensive scheduled maintenance event ($500K average, 2 weeks duration, 3000+ man-hours). It represents the highest maintenance cost per aircraft per year. Efficient execution directly impacts fleet availability and operating cost.

steps.description: Aircraft taxis into hangar at scheduled time. Fuel defueling, systems depressurization, safety locks applied. Initial walk-around inspection by lead mechanic.
Hangar supervisor assigns each task card to a qualified mechanic based on certifications, recency, and workload.
business_rules: Mechanic must hold valid type rating for this aircraft model
Mechanic must be current (< 90 days since last same-task sign-off)

steps.description: Mechanics perform assigned maintenance tasks following approved data (AMM, CMM, IPC). Findings (unexpected defects) are documented and flagged for inspector review.
Independent inspector reviews all findings and signs off critical tasks. Required for flight controls, landing gear, engines, and any task with a finding.
business_rules: Inspector cannot sign for work they performed
Inspector must hold higher authorization level than the mechanic

steps.description: Aircraft systems test (hydraulics, pneumatics, electrics). Engine dry crank and wet run. Flight control full deflection checks. All panels closed and secured.
Certifying staff reviews all completed work, verifies nothing is outstanding, and issues the Certificate of Release to Service. Aircraft exits hangar and returns to service.
business_rules: All task cards COMPLETED or DEFERRED
All findings dispositioned by inspector
Certifier holds valid EASA Part 66 license with type rating

trigger_conditions: Aircraft reaches C-check interval (flight hours, cycles, or calendar threshold)
Maintenance visit status changed to CONFIRMED

kpi_metrics.description: Total days from induction to CRS issuance
Percentage of visits completing on or before planned end date
Percentage of task cards with unexpected findings (high = poor pre-check condition assessment)
Inspector approval on first review without rework

glossary.definition: An emergency status indicating an aircraft is unable to fly due to a technical defect. Triggers highest-priority escalation: dedicated AOG desk, priority parts sourcing, management notification, and 24/7 resolution effort.
glossary.reason: AOG is the most urgent operational state in aviation. Every hour of AOG costs $10K-$150K in lost revenue, passenger disruption, and recovery costs. Airlines have dedicated AOG desks and procedures that bypass all normal processes.
glossary.agent_notes: If someone says 'AOG', the agent must respond with urgency. This is NOT a routine query. Ask: which aircraft, what part is needed, where is the aircraft. Escalate if no resolution within 2 hours.

glossary.definition: A legal document, signed by authorized certifying staff, confirming that maintenance has been performed correctly, using approved data and serviceable parts, and that the aircraft or component is fit for service. EASA Form 1 or FAA Form 8130-3.
regulatory_reference: FAA Part 43.9 - Content of maintenance records
EASA Part M.A.801 - Aircraft release to service
glossary.reason: The CRS is the legal permission to fly. Without it, no aircraft can depart after maintenance. The CRS is the final output of every maintenance visit and the proof of airworthiness that regulators and lessors demand.
glossary.agent_notes: CRITICAL CONCEPT: The agent must understand that generating a CRS is a legally binding act with consequences. Never suggest generating a CRS unless all maintenance is actually complete. If someone asks 'can the aircraft fly?', check if there's a valid CRS for the last maintenance visit.

glossary.definition: A regulatory-approved document that permits an aircraft to operate with specific items of equipment inoperative for a defined period. Each MEL item has a repair category (A, B, C, or D) that determines the maximum time before rectification is required.
glossary.reason: No aircraft is 100% serviceable at all times. The MEL provides a safe, regulated framework for dispatching aircraft with known defects. It balances operational necessity against safety by imposing strict time limits and operational restrictions per defect.
glossary.agent_notes: MEL category determines urgency: A=fixed at gate in days, B=fixed at station in days, C=fixed within 10-120 days, D=fixed at next check. The agent must know the category and track the deadline. EXPIRED MEL items ground the aircraft.

glossary.definition: A component with a mandatory maximum operating life (in flight cycles, flight hours, or calendar time) set by the OEM and approved by the regulator. The part must be permanently removed from service upon reaching its limit, regardless of its physical condition.
glossary.reason: LLP failures can be catastrophic: uncontained engine failure, landing gear collapse, rotor burst. These parts are life-limited because material fatigue is inevitable and non-detectable by inspection. Exceeding the limit is both a regulatory violation and a safety hazard that can cause fatalities.
glossary.agent_notes: Zero tolerance on LLP limits. If remaining_life <= 0, the aircraft is grounded. No exceptions, no extensions. Period. If a user asks 'can we fly just one more cycle?', the answer is NO.

glossary.definition: A legally enforceable regulation issued by the aviation authority (EASA, FAA, etc.) requiring a specific inspection, modification, or inspection on an aircraft or component. ADs are mandatory and have compliance deadlines.
glossary.reason: ADs are how regulators enforce safety fixes across the fleet. An AD may require a one-time inspection, a repetitive check, or a permanent modification. Missing an AD deadline is a regulatory violation that grounds the aircraft.
glossary.agent_notes: ADs are not optional. If an aircraft has an overdue AD, it cannot fly. The agent should proactively check AD compliance when discussing aircraft status.

use_cases.description: When an aircraft goes AOG with a known defective part, the maintenance controller must locate a serviceable replacement anywhere in the network (own stock, partner airlines, or vendors) and arrange emergency logistics to get the part to the aircraft within hours.

feature.description: Track serialized high-value components through their lifecycle with pool allocation, repair history, and current location across the network
feature.reason: Rotables represent the majority of MRO inventory capital; optimized pool management reduces spare asset investment and prevents stockouts

use_cases.reason: Every hour of AOG costs $10K-$150K. The difference between a 4-hour fix and a 24-hour fix can be $1M+. Rapid parts sourcing during AOG is the single highest-value use case for an AI agent in MRO.
use_cases.trigger: Aircraft.status changes to 'AOG' AND a defective part is identified and removed
steps: Identify the failed part number from the defect or removal record
Query rotable_component for SERVICEABLE instances of this part number across the network
If found at same station: dispatch immediately to hangar bay
If found at another station: calculate fastest logistics (courier, line bus, deadhead crew)
If no internal stock: query vendor pool for emergency AOG purchase
If no vendor stock: check if an equivalent or superseded part number can substitute
If no equivalent: check aircraft in STORED status for cannibalization potential (requires Chief Engineer approval)
If still no source: escalate to VP Maintenance and Procurement
business_rules: Cannibalization (removing part from another aircraft) requires written Chief Engineer approval
AOG procurement bypasses normal PO process (no price negotiation, any vendor with stock)
Logistics priority = highest (AOG courier, priority cargo, or airline crew deadhead)
Part must be traceable back-to-birth — cannot install without valid release certificate
If cannibalizing from stored aircraft: aircraft cannot be stored with missing LLPs
use_cases.success_condition: Serviceable part arrives at AOG aircraft and is installed within target time
use_cases.escalation_condition: No part sourced within 4 hours → escalate to VP Maintenance. 8 hours → escalate to COO.
use_cases.agent_training_notes: This is THE most critical use case. The agent must check all sources rapidly and in the right order: own stock → partner → vendor → cannibalization. Escalate early. Never let the controller waste time searching manually.

decisions.description: Determines whether a request to extend a deferred defect beyond its current MEL limit can be approved, and by whom.

feature.description: Manage minimum equipment list items with categorization, expiry tracking, operational restrictions, and rectification workflow
feature.reason: Allows continued safe operation with inoperative non-critical equipment while ensuring defects are fixed within permitted timeframes

input_conditions: requested_extension_days must not exceed MEL category max extension (category-specific)

approval_rules.notes: Short extensions on low-urgency items are routine
Category A/B items are high urgency — only Chief Engineer can extend
Safety-critical items CANNOT be extended under any circumstances
Prevent unnecessary cancellations if safe to defer

decisions.agent_training_notes: The agent must evaluate these rules in order. Never auto-approve Category A/B extensions — they always need a person. Never extend safety-critical items. If extension prevents a cancellation AND is safe, allow it.

alerts.description: Monitors all LLPs across the fleet and triggers alerts at configurable thresholds of life consumed. Three severity levels correspond to increasing urgency.

feature.description: Track parts with fixed life limits by serial number, monitor remaining hours/cycles/calendar time, and enforce removal before expiry
feature.reason: Prevents catastrophic failures and ensures full traceability for regulatory audits and asset resale value

severity_levels.action: Notify component engineer to plan replacement at next convenient maintenance slot. Generate shopping cart for replacement part.
Alert maintenance planner and MCC. Schedule dedicated shop visit. Verify replacement part is on order or in stock. No further deferral possible.
AIRCRAFT GROUNDED. Part must be replaced before next flight. Notify MCC, Fleet Manager, and Chief Engineer immediately. Regulatory non-compliance if flown.

alerts.affected_query: SELECT a.tail_number, llp.part_serial_number, p.part_number, p.part_name, llp.remaining_life, llp.total_life_limit, ((llp.total_life_limit - llp.remaining_life)::decimal / llp.total_life_limit) * 100 AS pct_consumed FROM life_limited_part llp JOIN aircraft a ON llp.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id JOIN part_master p ON llp.part_master_id = p.part_id WHERE ((llp.total_life_limit - llp.remaining_life)::decimal / llp.total_life_limit) * 100 >= 50 ORDER BY pct_consumed DESC
alerts.agent_notes: CRITICAL: EXPIRED alert = aircraft cannot fly. Period. No exceptions. The agent must recognize that an LLP with <= 0 remaining_life means the aircraft is grounded by regulation. Notify the appropriate people immediately.
